title: Intermediate Project
summary: Build a CLI task manager with persistence, error handling, and modular architecture

sections.title: The Task Manager Architecture
sections.content: This project brings together modules, file I/O, error handling, OOP, and functional programming into a real CLI application. The architecture follows a three-layer design: data layer (file-based JSON persistence), business logic (task CRUD operations), and presentation (CLI interface). Each layer is a separate module. Separating concerns like this means you can swap the file storage for a database later without touching the business logic or CLI code.

sections.title: Task model with validation
sections.content: Defines a Task class with validation in the constructor and a static fromJSON method

sections.title: Separation of Concerns Is the Goal
sections.content: The biggest leap from beginner to intermediate is understanding that code organization matters as much as functionality. In this project, storage.js should only read and write files — it should not know what a Task is. taskService.js should only manage business rules — it should not know about files or CLI formatting. cli.js should only handle user input and output. This modularity makes each piece testable, replaceable, and understandable on its own.

sections.title: Tight coupling between layers
sections.content: The #1 mistake intermediate developers make is letting concerns bleed across layers. Wrong: reading a file in the CLI module and parsing JSON there. Correct: storage module returns and accepts plain objects; business logic layer handles conversion to/from Task instances; CLI only calls business methods. Use dependency injection — pass the storage instance into the service constructor — so each component is independently testable.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Build the full task manager. Create three files: storage.js (read/write tasks.json, handles missing file), taskService.js (addTask, listTasks, toggleDone, deleteTask, uses storage), and cli.js (reads command line args, calls service, prints output). Support commands: 'add "Buy milk" high', 'list', 'done <id>', 'delete <id>'. Handle errors gracefully at each layer.
hints: How should storage.js handle the first run when tasks.json does not exist?
Where does JSON parsing/stringifying happen — in storage, service, or CLI?
How does the CLI extract the command and arguments from process.argv?
